envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Repeat business-to-business job revenue, usually quoted per file, plate, project, run, unit, or finishing operation, with some standing account relationships and volume schedules.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive in-plant departments, full-service printers whose support work is incidental to printing, software-only design platforms, equipment vendors, non-control financing vehicles, and firms outside the EBITDA band.
lens.included_activities: Independent US lower-middle-market providers of recurring outsourced prepress and postpress services to printers, publishers, packaging converters, agencies, and other external customers, including data imaging, platemaking, typesetting, print preparation, trade binding, finishing, and related production support.

a.bridge: BLS measures occupations for the broader printing subsector and nationally rather than wage-weighted tasks in independent NAICS 323120 firms. The range assigns high AI exposure to file intake, typesetting, image preparation, preflight, proof comparison, estimating, scheduling, and customer communication but low exposure to physical binding, cutting, folding, coating, setup, maintenance, and material handling; the mixed prepress/postpress population makes the direction of aggregate error uncertain.
caveats: No six-digit occupational or task distribution was located.
The code combines digital prepress with physically intensive postpress operations, so firm-level exposure will be bimodal.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted task census from at least fifteen independent support firms, stratified between prepress, platemaking, bindery, finishing, customer service, estimating, scheduling, and management, with current automation documented.
a.rationale: Support activities contain more digital information work than printing itself, especially in prepress, while trade binding and finishing remain physical. BLS projects prepress technicians down 14.6% and binding/finishing workers down 16.1% from 2024 to 2034, consistent with continuing automation, though those projections do not isolate generative AI or current unautomated tasks.

d5.bridge: National BLS employment projections for prepress and binding/finishing occupations proxy service quantity, even though employment also reflects productivity and the occupations appear in printers as well as independent support firms. Projected declines are tempered because labor can fall faster than constant-quality output under automation; conversely, insourcing by printers can reduce independent-provider demand faster than end-market print volume.
caveats: Employment is not a direct measure of constant-quality service demand.
The code's prepress and postpress segments may follow materially different demand paths.
d5.change_evidence: Five years of inflation-adjusted independent-provider sales and physical job counts by service line from Census product data, trade associations, and a panel of prepress and trade-finishing firms, separated from productivity and price effects.
d5.rationale: Customers will continue to need file preparation and physical finishing, but automated preflight, template-based composition, digital workflows, press-integrated finishing, consolidation, and insourcing reduce externally purchased support volume. Complex, short-run, specialty, overflow, and rush work supports a substantial remaining market.

caveats: Eligibility has not been observed directly at six digits.
The provided firm count is an ESTIMATE based on a margin bridge and may include nontransferable or captive-like operations.
e.change_evidence: A firm-by-firm screen of the 57 estimated candidates covering customer concentration, external revenue share, recurring-order share, owner dependence, equipment age, lease obligations, normalized EBITDA, and service mix.
e.rationale: The frozen count is already bridged to the EBITDA band. Independent support firms commonly serve external repeat customers, but some are too dependent on one printer, owner craft skill, obsolete finishing equipment, or declining legacy services to be transferable. Other establishments may function as effectively captive affiliates despite separate legal ownership.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 323120 ($944,779,731) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 323120 ($3,090,574,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1465 (margins.json[32]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Paper/Forest Products EBITDA/Sales 14.65% (mid-range for wood/paper/plastics)); revenue/employee $175,093 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 323120); class <5: 511 firms, avg revenue/firm $313,552 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $45,935 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 148 firms, avg revenue/firm $881,419 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $129,128 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 117 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,682,547 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $246,493 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 132 firms, avg revenue/firm $5,159,871 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $755,921 -> out of $1-10M band; class 100-499: 38 firms, avg revenue/firm $24,537,842 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $3,594,794 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 19 firms, avg revenue/firm $52,078,947 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $7,629,566 -> IN $1-10M band; n_band = 57

caveats: Operator displacement is likely much higher in routine prepress than in physical trade binding.
Software may automate the interface while an unseen production operator remains accountable downstream.
o.change_evidence: Printer and publisher procurement data showing the share of support spend shifted over five years to in-house workflow, software self-service, press-integrated finishing, offshore vendors, and independent operators.
o.rationale: Physical binding and finishing require an accountable operator, and complex prepress still needs responsibility for specifications and release-to-production quality. Yet routine preflight, typesetting, imposition, and versioning can move into customer self-service software or printer-integrated workflows, eliminating the independent support operator even when the underlying task persists.

caveats: No representative contract or repricing dataset was located.
Urgent and technically complex work may retain much more value than commodity file preparation or finishing.
q.change_evidence: Matched quote and invoice histories for repeated jobs before and after automation, with renewal timing, customer concentration, win/loss data, service-level changes, and gross-margin retention over three years.
q.rationale: Per-job quotes allow a provider to keep near-term labor savings, particularly when it solves rush, complex, or quality-sensitive work. However, printers and publishers can compare vendors, renegotiate standing accounts, bring digital prepress in-house, and demand price reductions; these forces pass a substantial share of gains through over five years. Faster turnaround and lower error rates are more defensible than pure unit-cost savings.

caveats: Industry evidence describes automation priorities, not achieved AI substitution.
Implementation economics differ sharply between file-based prepress and machine-based postpress.
rho.change_evidence: Twelve-month controlled deployments at prepress and trade-finishing firms reporting labor minutes per job, remake rates, spoilage, on-time delivery, exception escalation, and customer claims before and after implementation.
rho.rationale: Digital prepress tasks are already performed in structured files and workflow systems, making AI-assisted intake, preflight, versioning, proof checks, imposition, estimating, and exception handling feasible within five years. Adoption will be slowed by legacy workflow integration, poor job metadata, customer-specific tolerances, intellectual-property controls, and the high cost of an error that propagates into a print run. Physical finishing exposure is smaller and may require capital equipment rather than AI alone.

s5.bridge: Gallup found 22% of US employer-business owners across industries planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years. Discounting intentions for failed sales, internal transfers, liquidations, and nonqualifying reorganizations yields the base; mature printing-support ownership may create more exits than the all-industry population, but rapid technological obsolescence can turn exits into closures instead of control transfers.
caveats: Gallup is not industry- or size-band-specific and measures plans rather than completed transfers.
Family and employee transfers may not qualify as external control changes.
s5.change_evidence: Closed-deal and closure records for NAICS 323120 from printing-industry brokers, Axial, GF Data, PitchBook, SBA loan files, and state business registries, matched with owner age and succession status.
s5.rationale: A mature, fragmented supplier base should generate succession and consolidation opportunities, but the transferable set is smaller than the set of owners intending to exit. Specialized customer knowledge, owner-held relationships, and equipment condition can prevent a planned exit from becoming a going-concern sale.

principal_driver: Digital job intake and exception-heavy prepress workflows create a substantial AI-addressable labor surface in a high-labor-share industry.
principal_weakness: Routine support tasks can be automated or insourced entirely, reducing demand for the independent operator rather than merely lowering its labor cost.
